basic_info.name: Cleaning Product Formulation
basic_info.description: HSP matching between soil/stain types and surfactant/solvent cleaning systems predicts cleaning efficacy and guides formulation optimization. By characterizing the HSP of target soils (greases, oils, waxes, inks, protein-based stains) and matching them with cleaning agents, formulators can rationally select solvents, co-solvents, and surfactants that maximize soil removal. This approach is increasingly important for developing effective green cleaning products that replace hazardous solvents while maintaining performance.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Cleaning efficacy fundamentally depends on the thermodynamic affinity between the cleaning agent and the soil to be removed. When the HSP of the cleaning solvent or the hydrophobic tail environment of a surfactant micelle closely matches the HSP of the soil, dissolution or emulsification is thermodynamically favorable. The Ra distance between soil HSP and solvent HSP predicts the cleaning power: smaller Ra means the soil dissolves more readily in the cleaning medium. For surfactant-based systems, the effective HSP of the micellar interior (dominated by the hydrophobic tail structure) determines which soils can be solubilized. Mixed solvent systems and hydrotrope-assisted formulations can be optimized by adjusting the blend HSP to match diverse soil types. The approach enables a two-step strategy: first identify the soil HSP, then screen or blend solvents/surfactants to minimize Ra.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_soil-dD_solvent)^2 + (dP_soil-dP_solvent)^2 + (dH_soil-dH_solvent)^2); RED = Ra/R0_soil; Cleaning efficiency inversely proportional to RED
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 for effective soil removal; RED < 0.8 for rapid cleaning

required_inputs: Soil/stain HSP (dD, dP, dH) - varies by soil type
Cleaning solvent or surfactant system HSP (dD, dP, dH)
data_sources: HSPiP database for solvents, surfactants, and common soils
Published HSP data for oils, greases, waxes, and polymeric soils
Experimental soil characterization via solubility testing in graded solvents
Group contribution methods for novel soil/surfactant HSP estimation
complementary_parameters: Surface tension and contact angle (wetting behavior)

limitations: Real-world soils are complex mixtures with heterogeneous HSP, making a single HSP value an oversimplification
Cleaning involves kinetic factors (diffusion, mechanical action, rinsing) not captured by thermodynamic HSP matching
Surfactant micelle HSP is concentration-dependent and difficult to characterize precisely
Soil adhesion to substrates involves surface energy and mechanical interlocking beyond HSP
Protein-based and enzymatic soils require biochemical action not predicted by HSP
Aged or cross-linked soils (baked-on, oxidized) have different HSP than fresh equivalents
known_failure_modes: Enzymatic cleaning where biological activity dominates over solubility (e.g., protease for blood stains)
Particulate soils (clay, soot) where mechanical removal and charge interactions dominate
Polymerized or cross-linked soils (dried paint, baked-on food) where dissolution kinetics are extremely slow despite favorable HSP
Multi-layer soils where outer layer HSP differs from inner layer, requiring sequential cleaning strategies
validation_method: Gravimetric soil removal measurement (weight loss of soiled substrate)
Visual and instrumental cleanliness assessment (gloss, reflectance)
Contact angle measurement before and after cleaning
Standardized cleaning efficacy tests (ASTM D4488, DIN EN ISO 11998)

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Abbott, S.; Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters in Practice (HSPiP) software documentation

key_references.author: Archer, W.L.
key_references.title: Determination of Hansen solubility parameters for selected cellulose ether derivatives

literature.regulatory_relevance: HSP-guided formulation supports REACH compliance by enabling substitution of hazardous solvents (chlorinated solvents, n-hexane, toluene) with safer alternatives. Relevant to EPA Safer Chemical Ingredients List (SCIL) and EU Ecolabel criteria for cleaning products. FDA compliance for food-contact surface cleaners. VOC emission regulations drive reformulation toward low-VOC cleaning agents where HSP matching ensures efficacy is maintained.
